---
title: "Lesson 4: Two-Sample Confidence Intervals for Means"
subtitle: "Statistical Estimation for Data Science and AI"
---


::: {.column-margin}
{{< video 01_confidence-intervals-for-the-difference-between-population-means.mp4 >}}

[Transcript](01_confidence-intervals-for-the-difference-between-population-means.en.txt)
[Handout](02_video-slides-for-confidence-intervals-for-the-difference-between-population_module4_lesson4.pdf){width=700 height=980}
:::

## Slides on CIs for the Difference Between Population Means {#sec-confidence-interval-difference-means}

Suppose that: 
$X_{1,1},X_{1,2},\ldots,X_{1,n_1} \overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(\mu_1,\sigma_1^2)$
and
$X_{2,1},X_{2,2},\ldots,X_{2,n_2} \overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(\mu_2,\sigma_2^2)$
Assume that $\sigma_1^2$ and $\sigma_2^2$ 
are known and that the two samples are independent. 
The goal is to construct a $100(1-\alpha)\%$ confidence interval for
$\mu_1-\mu_2$

### Step One: Choose an Estimator {#sec-difference-means-estimator}

A natural estimator of $\mu_1-\mu_2$ is the difference between the sample means

$$
\widehat{\mu_1-\mu_2}=\overline{X}_1-\overline{X}_2
$$

### Step Two: Find the Distribution of the Estimator {#sec-difference-means-distribution}

Because the observations are normally distributed,

$$
\overline{X}_1 \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(\mu_1,\frac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1}\right)
$$

and

$$
\overline{X}_2 \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(\mu_2,\frac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}\right)
$$

The mean of the difference is

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[\overline{X}_1-\overline{X}_2]
&=
\mathbb{E}[\overline{X}_1]
-
\mathbb{E}[\overline{X}_2] \\
&=
\mu_1-\mu_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the two samples are independent, the variance of the difference is

$$
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{Var}(\overline{X}_1-\overline{X}_2)
&=
\operatorname{Var}\!\left(\overline{X}_1+(-1)\overline{X}_2\right) \\
&=
\operatorname{Var}(\overline{X}_1)
+
\operatorname{Var}\!\left((-1)\overline{X}_2\right)
&& \text{independence} \\
&=
\operatorname{Var}(\overline{X}_1)
+
(-1)^2\operatorname{Var}(\overline{X}_2) \\
&=
\operatorname{Var}(\overline{X}_1)
+
\operatorname{Var}(\overline{X}_2) \\
&=
\frac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1}
+
\frac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore,

$$
\overline{X}_1-\overline{X}_2
\sim
\mathcal{N}\!\left(
\mu_1-\mu_2,
\frac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1}
+
\frac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}
\right)
$$

Standardizing gives the pivotal quantity

$$
Z
=
\frac{
\overline{X}_1-\overline{X}_2-(\mu_1-\mu_2)
}{
\sqrt{
\frac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1}
+
\frac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}
}
}
\sim
\mathcal{N}(0,1)
$$

### Step Three: Choose the Critical Values {#sec-difference-means-critical-values}

Let $z_{\alpha/2}$ denote the positive standard-normal critical value satisfying

$$
P(Z>z_{\alpha/2})=\frac{\alpha}{2}
$$

Then

$$
P\!\left(
-z_{\alpha/2}
<
\frac{
\overline{X}_1-\overline{X}_2-(\mu_1-\mu_2)
}{
\sqrt{
\frac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1}
+
\frac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}
}
}
<
z_{\alpha/2}
\right)
=
1-\alpha
$$

### Step Four: Isolate the Parameter {#sec-difference-means-isolate-parameter}

Solving for $\mu_1-\mu_2$ gives

$$
\begin{aligned}
-z_{\alpha/2}
\sqrt{
\frac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1}
+
\frac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}
}
&<
\overline{X}_1-\overline{X}_2-(\mu_1-\mu_2)
<
z_{\alpha/2}
\sqrt{
\frac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1}
+
\frac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}
} \\
\overline{X}_1-\overline{X}_2
-
z_{\alpha/2}
\sqrt{
\frac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1}
+
\frac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}
}
&<
\mu_1-\mu_2 \\
&<
\overline{X}_1-\overline{X}_2
+
z_{\alpha/2}
\sqrt{
\frac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1}
+
\frac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}
}
\end{aligned}
$$

::: {#thm-ci-difference-means-known-variances}
## Confidence Interval with Known Population Variances

For two independent normal samples with known population variances, a $100(1-\alpha)\%$ confidence interval for $\mu_1-\mu_2$ is

$$
\underbrace{
\color{blue}{\overline{X}_1-\overline{X}_2}
}_{\text{point estimate}}
\;\pm\;
z_{\alpha/2}
\underbrace{
\color{red}{
\sqrt{
\frac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1}
+
\frac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}
}
}
}_{\text{standard error}}
$$
:::

::: {#exm-county-placement-scores}
## County Placement Scores

Fifth-grade students from two neighboring counties took a placement examination. Scores in both counties are assumed to be normally distributed. From previous years of data, the population variances for Counties A and B are believed to be $15.3$ and $19.7$, respectively.

For County A,

$$
n_1=57,
\qquad
\overline{x}_1=77.2,
\qquad
\sigma_1^2=15.3
$$

For County B,

$$
n_2=63,
\qquad
\overline{x}_2=75.3,
\qquad
\sigma_2^2=19.7
$$

Find and interpret a $99\%$ confidence interval for $\mu_1-\mu_2$.

Since $1-\alpha=0.99$,

$$
\alpha=0.01,
\qquad
\frac{\alpha}{2}=0.005
$$

The critical value is obtained from the standard normal distribution

$$
z_{0.005}
=
\Phi^{-1}(0.995)
\approx
2.56
$$

In R, this critical value is obtained using `qnorm(0.995)`.

The confidence interval is

$$
\begin{aligned}
(\overline{x}_1-\overline{x}_2)
&\pm
z_{0.005}
\sqrt{
\frac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1}
+
\frac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}
} \\
&=
(77.2-75.3)
\pm
2.56
\sqrt{
\frac{15.3}{57}
+
\frac{19.7}{63}
} \\
&=
1.9
\pm
1.9515 \\
&=
(-0.0515,\,3.8515)
\end{aligned}
$$

This interval gives plausible values for the population mean difference $\mu_1-\mu_2$.

Because the interval contains $0$, the data are compatible with

$$
\mu_1-\mu_2=0
$$

Equivalently, it is plausible that

$$
\mu_1=\mu_2
$$
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
## Data Snooping

Do not change the confidence level after examining the interval merely to exclude $0$. Selecting the confidence level in response to the observed data invalidates the stated coverage interpretation.
:::

## Large-Sample Approximation {#sec-difference-means-large-sample}

For large independent samples, with $n_1>30$ and $n_2>30$, the unknown population variances may be replaced by the sample variances.

::: {#thm-ci-difference-means-large-samples}
## Approximate Large-Sample Confidence Interval

An approximate $100(1-\alpha)\%$ confidence interval for $\mu_1-\mu_2$ is

$$
\underbrace{
\overline{X}_1-\overline{X}_2
}_{\text{point estimate}}
\;\pm\;
z_{\alpha/2}
\underbrace{
\sqrt{
\frac{S_1^2}{n_1}
+
\frac{S_2^2}{n_2}
}
}_{\text{estimated standard error}}
$$
:::


## Reflection
